title: BRIDGE Production Act of 2025
plain_summary: The BRIDGE Production Act of 2025 makes the government sell offshore areas to oil and gas companies for drilling. The government must hold at least 26 lease sales over ten years and has specific deadlines to follow. Companies will pay lower fees to the government for drilling rights than they do now. If the government misses deadlines or fails to approve new drilling programs, backup plans will automatically start. This bill is designed to increase oil and gas production in US waters.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Bringing Reliable Investment into Domestic Gulf Energy Production Act of two thousand twenty five or the BRIDGE Production Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 Finding
sections.text: Congress recognizes that President Donald Trump signed Executive Order fourteen thousand one hundred fifty six on January twentieth, two thousand twenty five. This order declared a national energy emergency.

sections.title: Section 3 Offshore Oil and Gas Lease Sales
sections.text: This section defines key terms and sets requirements for oil and gas lease sales in ocean waters. An offshore lease sale means a government auction where companies can buy the rights to drill for oil and gas in specific ocean areas. These sales must follow the same rules and conditions as previous sales held in two thousand twenty and two thousand seventeen. If the government receives an acceptable bid for any area, they must give the lease to the highest bidder within ninety days. The Secretary means the Secretary of the Interior. The Secretary can skip any legal requirement that would delay approving these lease sales. The government must hold at least twenty six offshore lease sales over ten years starting when this law takes effect. This includes twenty sales in the Gulf of America and six sales in the Cook Inlet Planning Area near Alaska. For Gulf of America sales, the government must hold at least one sale by specific dates every six months from August thirty first, two thousand twenty five through March thirty first, two thousand thirty five. For Cook Inlet sales, the government must hold at least one sale by specific dates roughly every two years from August thirty first, two thousand twenty five through March thirty first, two thousand thirty four. Each Gulf of America sale must offer at least eighty million acres for lease, or all available acres if less than eighty million remain unleased. Each Cook Inlet sale must offer at least one million acres, or all available acres if less than one million remain unleased. The sales must cover the same ocean areas described in a two thousand sixteen government planning document. The Secretary must approve requests to combine wells from different oil and gas reservoirs within forty five days of receiving an application. This section also changes the royalty rates (the percentage of profits companies must pay the government) from at least sixteen and two thirds percent to at least twelve and a half percent. The Secretary must create a pilot program that reduces royalty rates to ten percent for the first seven years of production if companies start producing oil or gas within three years of getting their lease. Companies must apply for this reduced rate within sixty days of starting production. Only twenty five leases can get this reduced rate. The Secretary must report to Congress by December thirty first, two thousand thirty on how well this program works.

sections.title: Section 4 Compliance with Other Federal Law
sections.text: For two years after the last required lease sale is held, this section says that certain existing government studies and approvals will be considered sufficient to meet various environmental protection laws. This includes laws that protect endangered species, marine mammals, the environment, historic places, and coastal areas. Companies will not have to do new studies or get new approvals under these laws. The section specifically says that protective measures for a type of whale called Balaenoptera ricei will not apply to oil and gas operations in the Gulf of America.

sections.title: Section 5 Judicial and Administrative Review
sections.text: If the Secretary of the Interior fails to hold a required lease sale within ten days of the deadline, potential bidders can sue to force the government to hold the sale. If a court finds the Secretary failed to hold a sale, the court must order the Secretary to hold it within one hundred twenty days of the original deadline. The court can also appoint a special master (an independent person) to make sure the Secretary follows the order. If the Secretary still does not hold the sale, the court will impose fines starting thirty days after the failure, unless the Secretary proves the delay was beyond their control. The court can give the Secretary an extra thirty days if circumstances were truly beyond their control, but fines will start if they still fail to act. People must file any lawsuit within thirty days of when a sale was supposed to happen. A special master can monitor all actions needed to hold the sale, including advertising, publishing notices, and issuing leases to winning bidders. Any missed sale must be held within one hundred twenty days of the original date. The government must pay for the special master using funds from the Secretary of the Interior's office. Lawsuits challenging these activities cannot stop lease sales or delay the processing of leases or drilling permits. If a court finds a sale did not follow the law, they must send it back to the Secretary to fix the problem but cannot cancel the sale or stop new leases. People seeking court orders to stop lease sales must provide financial security to cover potential damages. All court cases about this section must follow specific rules for offshore lease sale disputes.

sections.title: Section 6 Continuous Leasing Programs
sections.text: This section changes existing law to ensure there are no gaps between five year leasing programs. The Secretary must approve each new five year program at least one hundred twenty days before the current program expires. To meet this deadline, the Secretary must publish a draft program at least twenty four months before the current program expires and complete all required reviews and approvals on time. If the Secretary fails to approve a new program by the deadline, an automatic backup schedule takes effect immediately. This backup schedule requires two region wide oil and gas lease sales each year in the Gulf of America (one in the Central area and one in the Western area) and one region wide sale each year in Alaska waters. These sales must include all unleased acres in the areas identified in a two thousand sixteen government planning document. The sales under this backup schedule do not need additional environmental studies because existing reviews are considered sufficient. If the government receives an acceptable bid for any area in these sales, they must issue the lease within ninety days.

sections.title: Section 7 Minimum Lease Sales
sections.text: Starting January first, two thousand thirty five, the Secretary can only approve a five year leasing program if it includes at least fifteen offshore lease sales during those five years. If a court decides a program does not meet this requirement, an automatic replacement schedule takes effect immediately. This replacement schedule requires ten lease sales in the Gulf of America region (two per year) and five region wide sales in Alaska waters over the five year period. Gulf of America sales must offer all unleased acres in the region and use the same terms as a two thousand twenty sale, except royalty rates cannot exceed eighteen and three quarters percent. The first Gulf sale must happen within six months of the replacement program starting, with additional sales every seven months after that. Alaska sales must offer all unleased acres across Alaska waters and use the same terms as a two thousand seventeen sale, except royalty rates cannot exceed sixteen and three quarters percent. The first Alaska sale must happen within twelve months of the replacement program starting, with additional sales every eleven months. Sales under this replacement schedule do not need additional environmental studies or reviews. The existing environmental reviews from this law are considered sufficient to meet all federal environmental requirements. The Secretary can waive any requirement that would delay holding these sales. If the government receives an acceptable bid for any area, they must issue the lease within ninety days.
